data.title: Data augmentation of Python code refactoring datasets based on LLMs

RQa.note: The solution is described in an academic journal paper.
RQa.evidence: The Journal of Systems and Software 241 (2026) 113009

RQb.note: The solution is a domain-specific approach for augmenting Python code refactoring datasets to support tasks like detection, recommendation, and prioritization.
RQb.evidence: We focused on data augmentation techniques that involve code transformation instead of directly augmenting the features. We consider 4 main approaches based on: back-translation using a different programming language (more precisely, Java), back-translation from textual descriptions, code generation based on existing comments, and code generation from specifications obtained using CodeXGLUE on the refactored code.

RQc.note: The paper is published as open access, and models used (Gemini, GPT-4o, etc.) are commercial, but the proposed augmentation technique itself does not specify a cost model.
RQc.evidence: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license

RQd.note: The paper highlights that reliably assessing semantic equivalence in generated code is difficult, and automated validation may not catch all behavioral discrepancies.
RQd.evidence: the semantic preservation results should be interpreted cautiously. The lower agreement scores observed for functionality preservation highlight the difficulty of reliably assessing behavioral equivalence in LLM-generated code transformations, particularly when validation relies on static inspection and LLM-based reasoning.

RQe.note: The solution is presented as an experimental approach with findings on its effectiveness, and is not described as a production-ready tool.
RQe.evidence: Our findings suggest that many augmented samples remain semantically aligned with the original functions while adding diversity that improves robustness, reduces overfitting, and enhances generalization in automated refactoring detection.

RQf.note: The paper provides a link to a data replication package, but does not mention other community support channels like forums or chat communities.
RQf.evidence: I have shared the link to our replication package in the Data Availability section (https://figshare.com/s/5df5df00f39388dd35e6).

RQg.note: The solution is targeted at software engineering researchers and practitioners who build automated refactoring detection models.
RQg.evidence: This study examines how data augmentation can strengthen refactoring detection models.

RQh.note: The validation is based on experiments using a Python refactoring dataset and classifiers, not on industry collaboration or large-scale user studies.
RQh.evidence: Through extensive experimentation, we show that LLM-generated data can significantly improve model generalization, producing more stable and reliable refactoring-effort predictions.

IC1.note: The paper is explicitly situated within software engineering, specifically focusing on software refactoring, quality, and maintenance for code construction.

IC2.note: The solution applies large language models (Gemini, GPT-4o, DeepSeek, GPT-5-Nano) to augment data for refactoring detection, a software construction activity.

IC3.note: The paper describes the implementation and experimental evaluation of a new data augmentation framework, fitting the definition of a primary study.

IC4.note: The solution presents a functional pipeline and generates a validated dataset suitable for downstream tasks, as evidenced by the provided replication package and experiments.

IC5.note: The paper was accepted on 15 June 2026 and has a 2026 publication date, falling within the 2021-2026 timeframe.

EC4.note: The full text is written entirely in English.

EC5.note: The paper presents a primary investigation proposing and implementing a data augmentation technique, not a secondary or tertiary study such as a survey or review.

RQb.evidence: The proposed HLM method generates the embedding for each sub-tree according to hierarchies and collects the embedding of each sub-tree in context, to predict the next piece of code.

RQe.evidence: The proposed HLM method performs better than the state-of-the-art recurrent language model in dealing with the data inconsistency between training and testing, and achieves an average improvement in prediction accuracy of 11.2%.
